article_title: CAS Space Targets Late March for Kinetica-2 Maiden Flight, China's Latest Bid for a Reusable Medium-Lift Rocket

content_preview.title: CAS Space Targets Late March for Kinetica-2 Maiden Flight, China's Latest Bid for a Reusable Medium-Lift Rocket
content_preview.summary: Chinese Academy of Sciences spinoff CAS Space will attempt the first launch of its Kinetica-2 kerosene-LOX rocket from Jiuquan, carrying a prototype cargo spacecraft for the Tiangong space station.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

CAS Space, a commercial launch company spun out of the Chinese Academy of Sciences, is targeting late March 2026 for the maiden flight of its Kinetica-2 rocket from the Jiuquan Satellite Launch Center in China's Gobi Desert, according to [SpaceNews](https://spacenews.com/cas-space-to-launch-kinetica-2-in-late-march-carrying-prototype-cargo-spacecraft/). The mission will carry a prototype of the Qingzhou-1 cargo spacecraft, a lightweight resupply vehicle designed to support China's T...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News article at 498 words. Clear sections covering the mission, rocket specs, industry context, and open questions. Good 'What We Don't Know' section showing epistemic humility.
editor_notes.source_verification: 4 sources verified: SpaceNews (primary, authoritative space industry outlet), Space.com (established space news), Universe Discovery (secondary source), Next Spaceflight (rocket database). SpaceNews article confirms key technical claims including YF-102 engines, three core stages, 53m height, 12,000 kg LEO capacity, and late March timeline.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Core claims verified against SpaceNews snapshot: three 3.35m-diameter core stages with YF-102 engines (CASC-developed), ~12,000 kg to LEO, late March 2026 maiden flight. Minor discrepancy: article says '8,000 kg' SSO capacity (citing Universe Discovery) while SpaceNews says 'around 7,800 kg' — acceptable rounding given attribution to different source. Note: Next Spaceflight lists engines as 'Xuanyuan 2' and shows 2 stages + 2 boosters, conflicting with SpaceNews' 'three core stages with YF-102' — this is a source-to-source disagreement, not a fabrication. The article correctly follows its primary source (SpaceNews). Thrust figure of 766 metric tons matches Next Spaceflight's 7,515 kN (766 = 7515/9.807).
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and informative throughout. No sensationalism. Appropriate context about China's commercial space sector without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No existing articles on CAS Space or Kinetica-2. Nearest topic is SpaceX Starship (March 1) — completely distinct.
recommendations: Consider adding universediscovery.com and nextspaceflight.com to source allowlist as space reference databases
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Solid space industry News article with strong primary sourcing from SpaceNews. Technical details verified. Minor source discrepancies documented but do not affect publishability. Ready for publication.
